design_philosophy.core_loop: Jump across platforms, manage energy-based lightning attacks, select optimal pet companion for each stage, defeat boss at level end
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Resource management of Alisia's power meter balanced against continuous enemy pressure; pet selection strategy vs. stage hazards
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Playing as a powerful sorceress avenging her father while commanding loyal beast companions; combining elemental magic with agile platforming
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Energy depletion system forces deliberate, tactical attack pacing rather than button-mashing; pet companion system adds meaningful strategic layer to platformer formula; collaborates Japanese animation aesthetic (Gainax/Miyazaki influence) with Genesis hardware constraints

difficulty.lives_system: Alisia loses life bars from enemy attacks and traps; depleting all life bars requires spending a continue to restart current level
difficulty.continues_system: Limited continues; game ends when all continues exhausted
difficulty.checkpointing: Level-based checkpointing; no mid-level saves; no progress saving between sessions
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Player can select which of four pets (or none) to use at any time, allowing adaptive strategy; power-ups found in hidden locations provide optional difficulty mitigation; energy management mechanic gives skilled players tactical control

progression.upgrade_system: Power-ups scattered throughout first seven stages (mostly hidden) that: heal Alisia and pets, increase maximum life bars, improve attack power, grant temporary invulnerability; pets can be revived with 'Revive' power-ups after depletion
progression.unlock_mechanism: Eight sequential side-scrolling levels; each stage completed by defeating mandatory boss; final stage leads to ending cutscene
replayability_hooks: Performance scoring system tracks kills, Alisia's attack power level, and pet usage frequency
Hidden power-up locations encourage exploration and discovery on replays
Four distinct pet companions enable different playstyle approaches per stage
No save system creates speedrun/challenge-run potential for skilled players

world_building_techniques: Manual backstory: Alisia is daughter of sorcerer who fought Baldour (prince of evil); her father tortured to death before her eyes; she now possesses equal magical power and pursues Baldour's cocoon before resurrection
Opening: game 'goes straight into action' with minimal exposition
Ending cutscene: cinematic depiction of Alisia's triumphant return home
Level design influences: Gainax artwork reflects Miyazaki's Nausicaä aesthetic; sci-fi/fantasy hybrid (spaceships, robots alongside dragons, zombies)
Minimal dialogue; action-focused narrative structure typical of early 90s Genesis games

boss_design.philosophy: One mandatory boss per level serves as climactic encounter requiring mastery of stage mechanics and pet/energy management; boss encounters test player's tactical pet selection and power meter discipline

faction_indicators: Baldour (evil prince) as central antagonist representing corrupting magical force
Alisia's four beast companions (Dragon Frye, Boomerang Lizard, Thunder Raven, Ball O' Fire) as loyal faction opposing Baldour's corruption
danger_signals: Baldour's cocoon transport to Earth signals imminent catastrophic awakening
Ornah (Baldour's aide) as agent of resurrection threat
World remains 'not fully recovered from devastating effects of Baldour's last visit'
environmental_storytelling: Level design blends sci-fi and fantasy settings reflecting post-catastrophe world recovery
Miyazaki-influenced art direction suggests contaminated/transformed environments
Hidden power-ups imply dangerous territories requiring brave exploration
